1.synthesis:
At the summit of Sinai the Lord turns from sacrificial rites to agrarian legislation with a startling command: the land itself must observe a Sabbath. Every seventh year the fields are to lie fallow — no sowing, no pruning, no organized reaping. What springs up of itself in the sabbatical year belongs to all in common: owner, servant, hired worker, sojourner, and wild beast alike. Matthew Henry underscores the theological depth: "God having a particular propriety in this land, he would by this restraint keep them sensible of it." The sabbatical year was not agronomic rest alone; it was a yearly testimony that the land belongs to God and that Israel were but tenants under his sovereign lease.

Keil and Delitzsch observe that "the nation at large, with its labourers and beasts of burden, was to keep a Sabbath" each week, and now the land shares in that same pattern of rest: "the land shall keep a sabbath unto the LORD" (Lev 25:2). The command was simultaneously a faith test: Israel must trust that the earth's produce in the sixth year would supply three seasons — the sixth year, the fallow seventh, and the planting season of the eighth. Adam Clarke notes that the divine blessing on the sixth year, producing enough for three years, would itself be an incontrovertible miracle, authenticating the Mosaic legislation beyond dispute.

The law also democratized the land's produce in that seventh year. Matthew Henry enumerates its social purposes: teaching charity (the poor could glean freely from what grew of itself), liberation from covetousness, and a foretaste of the eschatological rest when all toil and struggle will cease.
1.historical_context: Keil and Delitzsch explain that the sabbatical year law applied once Israel entered and settled Canaan — the first seventh year came into operation approximately twenty-one years after entering the land, after the conquest and the seven-year land distribution under Joshua. The law in Leviticus 25 expands an earlier injunction in Exodus 23:10-11, adding detail about what constitutes "rest" and how the spontaneous produce is to be shared. Leviticus 25:1 specifies that the revelation came at or near Sinai, while Israel was still encamped before departing into the wilderness — thus the law was given early but applied late, structured for a settled agrarian community rather than desert wanderers.
1.literary_structure: Verses 1-7 form the opening unit of the chapter and function as the foundation for the grander jubilee legislation that follows. The structure parallels the weekly Sabbath command: six days of labor, rest on the seventh; six years of farming, rest in the seventh. Verse 2 states the principle ("the land shall keep a sabbath unto the LORD"), verses 3-5 spell out the specific prohibitions (no sowing, no pruning, no reaping the aftermath or unpruned vine), and verses 6-7 grant the positive provision (the land's spontaneous produce is for all, including cattle and wild animals). The rhythm of six-and-one, already embedded in Israel's week, now expands to govern the calendar of cultivation.
1.theological_application: Gill emphasizes that the sabbatical year taught several spiritual lessons simultaneously: dependence on divine providence; charity toward the poor who could glean freely; liberation from covetousness; and a foretaste of the eschatological rest. Matthew Henry adds that believers are thereby "eased of the burden of worldly care and labour, both being sanctified and sweetened to us, and we are enabled and encouraged to live by faith." The communal character of the sabbatical produce — freely available to all with no one locked out — was a yearly enacted parable of a society shaped by trust in God rather than accumulation against scarcity. Restraint in harvesting was not loss but an act of faith and generosity simultaneously.
1.christology: The sabbatical year pointed to the rest that Christ provides. Matthew Henry draws the connection: "This year of rest typified the spiritual rest which all believers enter into through Christ, our true Noah, who giveth us comfort and rest concerning our work." Hebrews 4 teaches that Christians enter a Sabbath-rest through faith in Christ, who ceased from his redemptive labors as God ceased from creation (Heb 4:9-10). The communal character of the sabbatical produce — free to all, shared without exclusion — anticipates the open invitation of the gospel: "Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters" (Isa 55:1; cf. Rev 22:17). Human life depends not on industry alone but on the word that proceeds from God (Deut 8:3).

8.synthesis:
After seven cycles of sabbatical years — forty-nine years in all — a fiftieth year is proclaimed by the blast of the ram's horn (yobel, from which "jubilee" derives) on the Day of Atonement. The trumpet sounds liberty throughout the land: every Israelite returns to his ancestral property, and every Hebrew bondservant returns to his family. Keil and Delitzsch note the profound symbolism of the announcement timing: "the blowing of trumpets... was the signal of the descent of the Lord upon Sinai, to raise Israel to be His people," and the jubilee trumpet repeats that founding note — liberty proclaimed again by the same Lord who called them out of Egypt.

The jubilee was not merely an economic reset but a declaration about the nature of covenant society: property is held in trust, not owned absolutely; persons cannot be permanently alienated from their inheritance. Matthew Henry observes that in the jubilee year "all sales and leases of land shall be void and of no effect, the land to return to the family of the first possessor." All land prices between jubilees were accordingly calculated from the number of remaining years — more years, higher price; fewer years, lower price — making every transaction essentially a long-term lease (Lev 25:15-16).

Verses 18-22 close the unit with a divine assurance: the land will produce enough in the sixth year to carry through the sabbatical year and into the year after planting. Three years of sustenance from one harvest — the jubilee's economic logic required a willingness to trust divine provision at the most basic level of daily bread.
8.historical_context: The jubilee was proclaimed on the Day of Atonement (Yom Kippur), the holiest day of the year, linking socio-economic liberation to the atonement rites of Leviticus 16. Keil and Delitzsch explain that the yobel — the ram's horn — was to be sounded "through the whole land," a simultaneous national proclamation. The institution of the jubilee is unique to Israelite law and has no direct parallel in ancient Near Eastern legislation, though the concept of debt release (andurārum) appears in Mesopotamian royal decrees issued at a king's accession. Verse 10 provides the charter statement: "You shall consecrate the fiftieth year, and proclaim liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants."
8.literary_structure: This section (vv. 8-22) divides naturally into three movements: the jubilee proclamation and its timing (vv. 8-12), the land-reversion principle with its pricing implications (vv. 13-17), and the divine guarantee of provision for the combined sabbatical-jubilee years (vv. 18-22). The central principle is stated in verse 23, which bridges into the next section: "the land is mine." The passage frames all property transactions entirely within the jubilee cycle, with verses 15-16 making the arithmetic explicit and verse 17 giving the ethical conclusion: "you shall not wrong one another."
8.theological_application: Gill notes that the jubilee proclamation on the Day of Atonement is theologically deliberate: liberation follows atonement. Just as the high priest emerged from the Holy of Holies on Yom Kippur to declare Israel's sins forgiven, the trumpet blast announced a broader liberation of persons and land. Matthew Henry observes that God's ownership of both land and persons is the ground of the jubilee: "The earth is the LORD's, and the fullness thereof," and Israel were but "strangers and sojourners with me" even in their own inheritance (v. 23). Adam Clarke emphasizes the miraculous provision promised for the dual sabbatical-jubilee years — the sixth-year harvest supplying three seasons — as a perpetual attestation of divine faithfulness requiring annual faith.
8.christology: The jubilee's proclamation of "liberty throughout the land to all its inhabitants" (Lev 25:10) is explicitly cited by Jesus in Luke 4:18-19, as he reads from Isaiah 61:1-2 in the Nazareth synagogue and declares: "Today this Scripture has been fulfilled in your hearing." Gill notes that the jubilee "was a type of the grace of the gospel, and of that spiritual liberty wherewith Christ makes his people free" (Gal 5:1). The announcement on the Day of Atonement prefigures the order of salvation: atonement first, then liberty — Christ's atoning death is the ground of every spiritual freedom his people enjoy. The economic rest of the jubilee anticipates the soul-rest promised by the one who is greater than Moses: "Come to me, all who labor and are heavy laden, and I will give you rest" (Matt 11:28).

23.synthesis:
The underlying principle of the jubilee — "the land is mine" (v. 23) — now unfolds in detailed legislation governing how land may be sold and redeemed between jubilee years. Because the land is the LORD's permanent possession and Israel holds it only in covenantal tenure, no sale can be final. If a man must sell family land through economic distress, the nearest kinsman-redeemer (gō'ēl) has first right to repurchase it; if no redeemer steps forward, the land reverts automatically at the jubilee. Matthew Henry frames the theological purpose: "God having a particular propriety in this land, he would by this restraint keep them sensible of it... to alienate their part of that land would be in effect to cut themselves off from their fellowship and communion with God, of which that was a token and symbol."

Verses 25-28 establish the redemption ladder: kin redeemer, then self-redemption if the original seller's fortunes recover, and finally jubilee reversion if neither occurs. The graduated structure reflects the same logic as the Boaz narrative in Ruth: the family member nearest in line bears the primary obligation, with each subsequent option a provision of grace. Verses 29-34 introduce the urban exception: houses in walled cities follow different rules — they revert only within a one-year window after sale — while Levitical cities are fully exempted, since the Levites hold no rural inheritance and their city houses are functionally their sole tribal stake.
23.historical_context: The gō'ēl system was foundational to Israelite social structure; the same institution governed property redemption, levirate marriage obligations, and the duty to avenge a murdered kinsman (Num 35:19). Keil and Delitzsch explain that the distinction between urban and rural property reflects the agricultural land's intimate connection to family sustenance and tribal identity: farmland was the economic and covenantal anchor of each tribe, while a town house was more a matter of residence. Levites received no tribal land allotment (Num 18:20), making their city houses functionally equivalent to the rural inheritance of other tribes — hence their perpetual redeemability.
23.literary_structure: Verse 23 states the theological axiom ("the land shall not be sold in perpetuity, for the land is mine"), and the rest of the section applies it in three cases: family land (vv. 24-28, with the full redemption ladder), urban houses (vv. 29-31, with the one-year rule), and Levitical city houses and fields (vv. 32-34, with perpetual redeemability and no alienation of suburban fields). The structure moves from the general principle to the specific pastoral case to the institutional exception, each governed by the same fundamental conviction that God holds ultimate title.
23.theological_application: Gill observes that the prohibition on permanent alienation taught Israel they were "strangers and sojourners" — a posture of dependence rather than absolute proprietorship. Matthew Henry draws a broader lesson: "The possessions of good people, who, having given up themselves to God, have therewith given up all they have to him, are in a particular manner at his disposal." The kinsman-redeemer obligation expressed the communal solidarity of covenant life: a brother's loss is the family's concern, and redemption of what is lost is a duty of love, not merely a market transaction. Adam Clarke notes that the legislation placed a permanent brake on the concentration of land in few hands, preserving the tribal distribution across generations.
23.christology: The kinsman-redeemer (gō'ēl) who restores alienated inheritance is a profound type of Christ. As Boaz redeemed Naomi's land and took Ruth as his bride (Ruth 4:1-10), Christ as our elder brother and nearest kinsman assumes our debt, restoring the inheritance forfeited by sin. Keil and Delitzsch's emphasis that land-alienation was also a severing from God's sanctuary prefigures the exile of mankind from God's presence through sin — and Christ's redemption as the restoration of that fellowship. The jubilee reversion, even without an active redeemer, speaks to God's sovereign purpose to restore what sin has scattered — a cosmic jubilee that Paul describes as the "summing up of all things in Christ" (Eph 1:10).

35.synthesis:
The jubilee's provisions for land extend naturally to provisions for persons. When a fellow Israelite falls into poverty — his hand "wavering" or "slipping," as Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew trembling hand of verse 35 — his neighbors bear a graduated responsibility to support him. Three obligations follow in sequence: to allow him to live alongside as a dependent (v. 35); to lend without interest — neither neshek ("bite," charged on money) nor tarbît ("increase," charged on food) — (vv. 36-38); and if he must sell himself into service, to treat him not as a slave but as a hired worker and sojourner (vv. 39-43). The jubilee year guarantees his release regardless of the contract terms.

Matthew Henry emphasizes the humanizing thrust: "God here specifies the duty which must prevent a brother's slavery." The passage draws a sharp line between Israelite and foreign bondservants: foreigners acquired abroad might be held permanently (vv. 44-46), but no Israelite may be enslaved in perpetuity by another Israelite. The theological ground is explicit: "I brought them out of the land of Egypt; they shall not be sold as slaves" (v. 42). The Exodus is not merely a historical memory but a living legal fact — God's redemptive act creates a permanent status that no subsequent debt can negate.
35.historical_context: The prohibition on lending at interest (neshek, "bite") to a poor brother reflected the non-commercial character of loans in Israelite society. Adam Clarke notes that usury extracted from a poor man was regarded as an act of oppression — taking life from someone already in distress. The grounding verse (v. 38: "I am the LORD your God who brought you out of the land of Egypt") echoes the preamble to the Decalogue, situating the economic command within the same covenantal framework as the Ten Commandments. The permission to hold foreign slaves (vv. 44-46) reflected the ancient world's reality while marking a clear distinction between covenant membership and those outside it — a distinction the New Testament ultimately abolishes in Christ (Gal 3:28).
35.literary_structure: The section divides into three subsections, each grounded in a theological premise: support without usury (vv. 35-38, grounded in the Exodus — "I am the LORD your God who brought you out of Egypt"); the hired-servant provision for impoverished Israelites (vv. 39-43, grounded in divine ownership — "they are my servants"); and the contrasting provision for foreign bondservants (vv. 44-46, grounded in the distinction between covenant and non-covenant people). Each subsection escalates the vulnerability of the Israelite described and deepens the corresponding obligation of the brother who has means.
35.theological_application: Gill notes that God's right over Israel — "they are my servants, whom I brought out of the land of Egypt" — is the ultimate ground of human dignity in the covenant community: God's ownership precedes any human claim. Matthew Henry applies this to the Christian: since Christ has purchased us with his own blood (1 Cor 6:20), we cannot treat ourselves or our brothers as mere economic units. The interest prohibition reflects that covenant economics differ from market economics: another's poverty is an opportunity for generosity, not profit (cf. 2 Cor 9:6-15). Adam Clarke finds here the root of what later prophets would call justice — structural protections against the powerful exploiting the vulnerable.
35.christology: The declaration "they are my servants whom I brought out of Egypt" (v. 42) points forward to the new exodus accomplished by Christ. Paul applies this logic directly: "You were bought with a price; do not become bondservants of men" (1 Cor 7:23). The jubilee release from debt-bondage becomes the pattern for the gospel release: Christ the true Kinsman-Redeemer pays our debt and sets us free — not temporarily until the next jubilee but permanently. "If the Son sets you free, you will be free indeed" (John 8:36). The prohibition on exploiting a poor brother mirrors Christ's identification with the vulnerable: "as you did it to one of the least of these my brothers, you did it to me" (Matt 25:40).

47.synthesis:
The chapter closes with its most intricate case: an Israelite who, having exhausted every other option, has sold himself into the household of a wealthy foreigner or sojourner resident in the land. This situation inverts the normal social structure — it is a Gentile who holds power over a covenant member. The passage insists this cannot be a permanent condition. Redemption remains available at any time: by a near kinsman (uncle, cousin, or any close relative), by self-purchase if the man's means recover, and if nothing intervenes, automatically at the jubilee. The price of redemption is calculated with precision: the original sale price, adjusted for the years of service already rendered and the years remaining until jubilee, so that the redemption cost accurately reflects the outstanding term of service (Lev 25:50-52).

Matthew Henry calls this "a kindness to the person sold, that though he was in the hands of a stranger, and a heathen too, yet his master could not keep him beyond the time limited by the law of jubilee." Keil and Delitzsch observe that the careful arithmetic of redemption demonstrates God's meticulous concern even for the most marginalized covenant member: no circumstance, however extreme, removes an Israelite from the reach of the jubilee's liberation. Verse 53 specifies the treatment conditions during service: "as a worker hired year by year he shall be with him" — the foreign master may not exercise the harsh authority of a slave-owner over one who belongs to God.
47.historical_context: The scenario envisions wealthy non-Israelites settled among the people — merchants, traders, or craftsmen who had prospered in the land. That such persons could accumulate wealth exceeding that of native Israelites is acknowledged without idealization. The law does not prohibit the economic arrangement but governs it with firm protections derived from covenant theology: the Israelite's status as God's servant supersedes his contractual obligations to any human master. Adam Clarke notes that the multiple redemption pathways reflect the law's determination that every legal avenue to restoration should remain open before the jubilee serves as the final safety net.
47.literary_structure: Verses 47-49 establish the scenario and list the order of potential redeemers (uncle, cousin, any near kinsman of his clan, or the man himself if his means recover). Verses 50-52 provide the arithmetic of graduated redemption proportional to years remaining. Verse 53 specifies treatment conditions. Verses 54-55 close with the unconditional jubilee release and the theological summary that functions as the chapter's conclusion: "For to me the people of Israel are servants. They are my servants whom I brought out of the land of Egypt: I am the LORD your God." The declaration answers every human claim of ownership over a covenant member with a prior, higher claim.
47.theological_application: Gill emphasizes that the jubilee's guarantee to the enslaved Israelite — even one held by a foreigner — demonstrates that God's covenant obligations to his people are not contingent on human circumstances. Matthew Henry draws the parallel for the believer: no earthly power, however wealthy or authoritative, can permanently hold in bondage those whom God has redeemed. Adam Clarke finds here a model of graduated redemption: multiple human avenues are provided before the final recourse of jubilee, reflecting divine patience and the genuine weight placed on human responsibility and family solidarity within the covenant community's redemptive framework.
47.christology: The closing verse — "for to me the people of Israel are servants... I am the LORD your God" (v. 55) — is the ground upon which the entire chapter's liberation theology rests, and it points to Christ's definitive act of redemption: "you are not your own, for you were bought with a price" (1 Cor 6:19-20). As the enslaved Israelite was bought out of Gentile bondage by a kinsman, so sinners alienated from God — "sold under sin" (Rom 7:14) — are redeemed by Christ the eternal Kinsman. The jubilee's arithmetic of redemption gives way to a purchase of infinite cost: "not with perishable things such as silver or gold, but with the precious blood of Christ" (1 Pet 1:18-19).
